project.name: Ad Data Ingestion Pipeline
project.scenario: The marketing team runs ads on 10+ media platforms and tunes budgets daily on spend, CTR, and CPA — the fresher the numbers, the better the calls.
project.pain: Data was pulled by hand from each platform's console and stitched into spreadsheets: 10+ hours a week, and at best day-old numbers — always making today's decisions on yesterday's data.
project.now: Every hour, the app pulls numbers from every ad platform, makes the field names match, keeps repeat records from being counted twice, and saves the clean result in a database. The team checks a dashboard themselves — manual exports no longer exist.

scheduler.name: Scheduler
scheduler.role: Kicks off an ad-data pull every hour, on the hour — no human needed.
impact: Data freshness went from daily to hourly; the numbers the team sees are at most an hour old.
No one runs scripts by hand anymore — that is where the 10+ saved hours a week come from.
scheduler.how: On the hour, AWS wakes a small piece of code that does exactly one thing: tell the Fetch Layer to start pulling numbers. Then it sleeps until the next hour.
scheduler.fail: If one run fails, that hour's data has a gap; the next run fires as usual — nothing cascades.

code.n: Tells AWS: when there is work, run the function src/ingest.run.
This line is the timer itself. cron(0 * * * ? *) means: fire at minute 0 of every hour.

code.c:
// src/ingest.ts
export const run = async () => pipeline.start();
code.n: The only thing it does when woken: start the pipeline.

scheduler.tourHint: Stop 1 · Starts the hourly pull — this is why the dashboard is never more than about an hour behind.

fetch.name: Fetch Layer
fetch.role: Pulls raw ad data from 10+ media platforms.
impact: 10+ platforms, each with its own way of sharing data — this is the part most likely to need future fixes.
If one platform changes its API, only that platform's connector breaks; the rest keep running.
Adding a new platform means adding one new connector that follows the same pattern — the rest of the app stays put.
fetch.how: There is one small connector per platform. Each connector gives the rest of the app the same kind of answer, even though TikTok, Google, and Meta all make you ask differently. An API is simply an agreed way for two programs to talk.
fetch.fail: If a platform's API is down or changed, only that platform's data is missing; the rest keep running, and the gap is backfilled next round.

code.n: One file like this per platform — it registers who it is.
Every connector has the same getData function, so the rest of the app can ask every platform the same way.
code.risk: ⚠ This line makes a real API call and uses part of that platform's allowed call budget. Redis slows calls down so the platform does not block you.

tradeoff.cost: Every platform's quirks are yours to maintain; when a platform changes, you patch that connector.
tradeoff.when: With only 2-3 major platforms and stable SDKs, SDKs are less work. With 10+ platforms that all need to feed the same dashboard, one connector pattern is easier to control.

fetch.tourHint: Stop 2 · Pulls raw numbers from 10+ platforms — this is the part most likely to need upkeep.

normalize.name: Field-name cleaner
normalize.role: Turns each platform's differently named fields into one shared format.
impact: "Impressions" on the dashboard always means one thing, whichever platform the data came from.
When a new platform uses odd field names, the fix stays here — the later parts do not need to move.
normalize.how: Platforms name the same metric differently: some say views, some say impressions. This layer renames fields against one mapping table, then passes the data on.
normalize.fail: A field missing from the mapping table is skipped and logged — unrecognized data is never passed downstream.

code.n: This is the translation list: platform names on the left, the app's shared field names on the right.

code.c:
export const normalize = (src, row) =>
  mapKeys(row, FIELD_MAP[src]);
code.n: This one line swaps each platform's field names into the app's shared names.

normalize.tourHint: Stop 3 · Field names get cleaned here, so the later parts do not care where the data came from.

redis.name: Redis safety check
redis.role: Keeps the same record from being counted twice and slows API calls down so platforms do not block you.
impact: The same ad record never enters the database twice, so report numbers do not get inflated.
No API key gets banned for calling too hard — one ban can mean days of missing data.
redis.how: Each record gets a fingerprint, like a short ID made from its important fields. Redis checks whether that fingerprint was seen before and drops the record if it was. Before every API call, Redis also checks how many calls were already made this hour; over the limit means waiting.
redis.fail: If Redis goes down, the app pauses the pull rather than calling platforms too aggressively — losing one hour is safer than getting blocked.

code.n: This keeps repeats out. NX means "save this only if it has not been seen before" — if Redis says no, the record is a duplicate.

code.c:
const used = await redis.incr('quota:' + platform + ':' + hour);
if (used > LIMIT) await sleepUntilNextHour();
code.n: This is the speed limit: count how many times we called this platform this hour; if it is too many, wait.

tradeoff.a: Redis for repeat checks and call speed limits
tradeoff.cost: One more moving part to run and watch.
tradeoff.when: At small volume, a database rule is enough and Redis can be removed. At 10+ platforms pulled hourly, Redis earns its keep.

redis.tourHint: Stop 4 · Repeat checks and speed limits — this keeps your API keys from getting blocked.

db.name: Database
db.role: Stores the cleaned ad data and gives the dashboard the numbers it asks for.
impact: Every number on the Dashboard comes from here — if it is down, reports go blank.
Data is stored by day: "last 7 days" is fast, "all of last year" is slow. That's the current design's boundary.
db.how: Data cleared by Redis is written row by row into the ads_daily table. Looker Studio connects directly and asks questions like "what was total spend last week?" The database answers by adding many saved rows into one chart number.
db.fail: If the database is unreachable, the app waits and tries again later — nothing is lost; the Dashboard shows older data meanwhile.

code.c:
CREATE TABLE ads_daily (
  platform    text,
  ad_id       text,
  date        date,
  impressions bigint,
  spend       numeric,
  PRIMARY KEY (platform, ad_id, date)
);
code.n: The whole store is one table. The three-part primary key means one ad can only have one row per day — the final guard against double-counting.
code.risk: ⚠ Changing this table's structure (columns, primary key) is the hardest thing in the project to undo — everything is built on it. Back up first.

tradeoff.cost: Past hundreds of millions of rows, big dashboard questions can get slow.
tradeoff.when: When the team runs "two years, all platforms" analyses or rows pass 100M, move to BigQuery. Until then PostgreSQL is cheaper and simpler.

db.tourHint: Stop 5 · Where the data finally lives — every Dashboard number comes from here.

dash.name: BI Dashboard
dash.role: Lets the marketing team see spend, CTR, and CPA directly — no SQL, no waiting for exports.
impact: The team self-serves; you never manually export a weekly report again.
This part is bought, not built — zero lines of frontend code.
dash.how: Looker Studio connects straight to PostgreSQL. You build charts by dragging fields around; Looker writes the database questions for you.
dash.fail: When a chart errors, the problem is almost always earlier in the flow — check whether today's data reached the Database before blaming the chart.
dash.tourHint: Stop 6 · The team checks numbers here themselves — this is the visible payoff.
